meta.subject: English - Grammar & Usage
meta.description: JAMB English Grammar and Usage questions covering idioms, vocabulary, tense, sentence structure, and more.

questions.question: In the match against the uplanders team, the sub mariners turned out to be the dark horse
questions.explanation: A 'dark horse' in sports refers to an unexpected winner—a team no one saw coming. The Submariners weren't favorites but won anyway, making them the dark horse.

questions.question: Only the small fry get punished for such social misdemeanors
questions.explanation: 'Small fry' is slang for insignificant or unimportant people. Just like small fish in a lake aren't valued, small fry refers to people without power or status.

questions.question: He spoke with his heart in his mouth
options: with a lot of confusion in his speech
without being able to make up his mind
questions.explanation: Having your 'heart in your mouth' means being extremely nervous or frightened. It's the feeling when fear overwhelms you—like your heart is literally jumping out.

questions.question: The leader in today's issue of our popular newspaper focuses on inflation
questions.explanation: In newspaper terminology, the 'leader' is the main opinion piece about current issues—this is called an editorial. It's where the newspaper's stance on topics like inflation is presented.

questions.question: From the ways my friend talks, you can see he is such a bore
questions.explanation: A 'bore' is someone uninteresting—someone whose conversation or company is dull and tiresome. You avoid bores because they're not engaging.

questions.question: His jail terms were to run concurrently
questions.explanation: 'Concurrently' means at the same time. If sentences run concurrently, they overlap—so a 2-year and 3-year sentence would be served at once (5 years total), not back-to-back.

questions.question: There is some obvious symmetry in the whole presentation
questions.explanation: 'Symmetry' refers to balance and harmonious arrangement. When something has obvious symmetry, it's well-organized and orderly—everything is proportionate and in its place.

questions.question: The bill has to wait as we are now insolvent
questions.explanation: 'Insolvent' is a legal/financial term meaning unable to pay debts—essentially bankrupt. It's the formal way to say someone or a company has run out of money.

questions.question: All his plans fell through
questions.explanation: 'Fall through' means to fail or collapse completely. When plans fall through, they don't happen—something went wrong and they fell apart.

questions.question: The balance sheet at the end of the business year shows that we broke even
options: had no money to continue business
were heavily indebted to our bankers
questions.explanation: 'Break even' is a business term meaning you made no profit and no loss—you recovered exactly what you spent. It's the neutral outcome between success and failure.

questions.question: He was appointed specifically to put the recruits through
questions.explanation: 'Put through' in this context means to train or prepare someone for their role. Recruits are put through training to prepare them for their duties.

questions.question: The result of his experiment represents a breakthrough in medical science
questions.explanation: A 'breakthrough' is a significant discovery or achievement. In medical science, a breakthrough is a major step forward—something that changes how we treat or understand disease.

questions.question: Being an optimist, our professor always sees the bright side of most things
questions.explanation: 'Bright side' doesn't mean literally bright—it means the positive aspect. An optimist sees the cheerful, hopeful side of situations even when things are difficult.

questions.question: The state government appointed a commission of inquiry to go into the community's complaints carefully and without prejudice
questions.explanation: 'Go into' here means to investigate thoroughly. The commission was formed to examine the complaints carefully—to look into what happened and why.

questions.question: The traffic situation in Lagos can lead to disastrous consequences; a man lost a very lucrative job because he was held up by it
questions.explanation: 'Held up' means delayed or stopped from reaching somewhere on time. The man arrived late for work or important meetings because of traffic, costing him his job.

questions.question: Mrs. Dada has been deserted by her husband because he feels she has a heart of stone
options: she has little warmth of feeling
questions.explanation: A 'heart of stone' means someone is cold, unfeeling, and shows no emotional warmth. The husband feels she lacks compassion and care, so he abandoned her.

questions.question: Mr. Jack could be a successful businessman if he paid more attention to the more intricate aspects of his account
options: Jack will have a very good chance of succeeding
Mr. Jack will find it difficult to succeed
Mr. Jack will succeed in spite of all odds
questions.explanation: The conditional 'could be...if he paid' suggests a hypothetical possibility. It means if Jack focuses on details, he'd have a very good chance of succeeding. He's not guaranteed to, but his chances are good.

questions.question: If my father had not arrived, I would have starved. This sentence means
options: My father did arrive and I didn't starve
I had to starve because my father didn't come
my father didn't arrive and I didn't starve
I should have starved but I didn't
I had starved before my father arrived
questions.explanation: This is a past conditional: 'If...had not...would have.' It means: Father DID arrive (so I DIDN'T starve). The condition describes what DIDN'T happen, and the consequence describes what also DIDN'T happen.

questions.question: The two sprinters were running neck and neck
questions.explanation: 'Neck and neck' means running side-by-side at exactly the same speed—neither gaining on the other. It's used in racing when competitors are perfectly matched.

questions.question: He stared at her
questions.explanation: 'Stare' means to look long and intently—stronger than a casual glance. 'Gaze' carries the same meaning: a prolonged, intense look, often expressing wonder or affection.

questions.question: When the man was caught by police, he presented a bold front
options: he walked up to the policemen
he faced the situation with apparent boldness
he bravely attempted to give them a present
he frowned at them in a defiant manner
questions.explanation: 'Present a bold front' means to appear confident and brave even if you're scared inside. It's showing the appearance of bravery to hide your real fear.

questions.question: I have been able to observe him at close quarters
options: within a short space of time
questions.explanation: 'At close quarters' means at a very close distance or range. If you observe someone at close quarters, you're watching them from nearby, seeing details clearly.

questions.question: At the site of the ghastly accident the poor woman's hair stood on end
options: the wind blew her hair upwards
questions.explanation: When your 'hair stands on end,' it means you're terrified or shocked. The horror of the accident frightened her so much that her fear was physical—literally hair-raising.

questions.question: When the beggar was tired, he .... down by the roadside
questions.explanation: 'Lay' vs 'lie' confusion is common. 'Lie' means to recline (no object). 'Lay' means to place something (takes an object). Here, the beggar reclined, so 'lay' (past tense of lie).

questions.question: He did not like .... leaving the class early
questions.explanation: After a verb like 'like,' we use the possessive form 'our' when followed by a gerund (leaving). It's 'our leaving,' not 'us leaving'—the possessive modifies the action.

questions.question: Before the operation, the dentist found that his patient's teeth....
questions.explanation: 'Found' is past tense, so what happened before must also be past: 'had long decayed.' This describes a state that existed prior to the discovery.

questions.question: The employer, not the salesman and his representative .... responsible for the loss
questions.explanation: 'Not...but the employer' means the employer is singular. When subjects are joined by 'not...but,' the verb agrees with the subject after 'but.' So: 'is responsible.'

questions.question: The boy was born before his parents actually got married and so the court has declared him ....
questions.explanation: 'Illegitimate' describes a child born outside marriage. 'Illegal' means against the law, 'illicit' means secret/forbidden, but 'illegitimate' is the specific legal term for birth status.

questions.question: As he was still owing me two naira, I was careful not to .... him any more money
questions.explanation: 'Lend' means to give money with expectation of return. 'Loan' is the noun form. Here we need the verb: she wouldn't lend him more because he already owed her.

questions.question: Last week the .... at the theater was the longest I had ever seen
questions.explanation: An 'audience' is specifically a group of people gathered to watch a performance. At a theater, you have an audience. A 'crowd' is general; a 'congregation' is for churches.

questions.question: If you travel by air you will be given an allowance to cover ....
questions.explanation: 'Luggage' and 'baggage' are uncountable nouns in English—they don't take articles or plural forms. You say 'the luggage' not 'the luggages,' just as you'd say 'the furniture' not 'furnitures.'

questions.question: Tennyson and Browning lived about the same time and therefore .... poets
questions.explanation: 'Contemporary' is an adjective meaning living or creating during the same time period. 'Contemporaneous' is similar but more formal; 'contemporary poets' means poets of the same era.

questions.question: The green snake which lives in the green grass is using a perfect ....
questions.explanation: 'Camouflage' means blending in with surroundings to hide. A green snake in green grass uses camouflage to avoid detection—it's the perfect example of natural disguise.

questions.question: In order to carry out necessary examination the dead body was ....
questions.explanation: 'Exhumed' means to dig up or unbury something. When they need to examine a buried body, they exhume it. 'Extradited' is about handing over criminals; 'extracted' is pulling something out.

questions.question: Remember this matter is strictly ....
questions.explanation: The preposition 'between' takes the object pronoun 'me,' not subject pronoun 'I.' It's 'between you and me,' not 'between you and I.' This is a common mistake.

questions.question: He went abroad with a view .... a business partner
questions.explanation: 'With a view to' is a set phrase that takes a gerund (verb+ing). So it's 'with a view to finding,' not 'with a view to find.' Gerunds follow this preposition.

questions.question: He had just entered the office .... the telephone rang
questions.explanation: 'When' connects two past events happening at the same moment. 'He had just entered...when the telephone rang' shows the timing—they happened almost simultaneously.

questions.question: They .... receive visitors on short notice.
questions.explanation: 'Used to' describes past habits that don't happen anymore. 'Are used to' means accustomed to. Here, 'used to receive' means they had a past habit of receiving unannounced visitors.

questions.question: His suggestion are completely .... the point and cannot be accepted
questions.explanation: 'Beside the point' is the correct idiom—it means irrelevant or not relevant to the discussion. 'Beside' means 'to the side of' or 'next to,' making suggestions miss the mark.

questions.question: For .... he is a secretary we shall not have correct minutes
questions.explanation: 'As long as' here means 'given that' or 'because.' It introduces a reason: 'Since he is a secretary, we should have correct minutes—if not, something's wrong.'

questions.question: He went up quickly and returned ....
questions.explanation: 'Fastly' isn't standard English—'fast' is an adjective AND an adverb. You say 'go fast' or 'return fast,' not 'return fastly.' 'Quickly' works, but 'fast' is the natural match.

questions.question: Invariably, he ends his letters .... amicably
questions.explanation: 'Yours' is the possessive pronoun (like his, hers, mine). It never has an apostrophe. 'Your's' is incorrect. 'Ending his letters yours amicably' means signing off as a friend.

questions.question: If I went to the cinema I .... early
questions.explanation: This is a present conditional (hypothetical). 'If I went' (conditional) requires 'would return' (conditional). You can't use 'shall return' (which is future) or 'am returning' (present).

questions.question: The traditional folktales form an .... to the book
questions.explanation: An 'appendix' is supplementary material added to the end of a book. Folktales as traditional content would logically be added as an appendix—extra material separate from the main text.

questions.question: the frightening explosion in the factory .... whole wing
questions.explanation: 'Wiped out' means completely destroyed or eliminated. An explosion that 'wiped out' a whole wing means it destroyed it entirely—nothing was left standing.

questions.question: Our plane was scheduled to .... at 10.00 hrs. but it was delayed because of the bad weather
questions.explanation: 'Take off' is the standard phrasal verb for airplanes beginning flight. 'Lift off' is spaceship terminology. Here, the plane was scheduled to depart at 10:00.

questions.question: As luck would have it, the weather brightened almost immediately and after six hours flight we .... at Heathrow Airport
questions.explanation: 'Touched down' is the correct phrasal verb for landing an aircraft. 'Came down' is vague; 'dropped' is too abrupt. Pilots 'touch down' when they land on the runway.

questions.question: Before we left the airport, each passenger was made to .... certain form
questions.explanation: 'Fill in' means to complete a form by writing information in the blanks. 'Write in' is too generic; 'enter in' would say the data goes into something else. 'Fill in a form' is standard.

questions.question: My father cannot bear the .... of the concoction that emanates from my mother's soup pot
questions.explanation: 'Smell' is the most direct term—the father 'cannot bear' (dislikes) the smell. 'Aroma' has positive connotations (pleasant smell); 'scent' is more delicate. The harsh 'smell' fits the complaint better.

questions.question: I would not have condescended to appease the traffic policeman but I happened .... traffic regulation
questions.explanation: 'Happened to have broken' is past infinitive—'I happened to have broken a traffic regulation.' It's past relative to 'happened,' showing something that occurred before the appeasement.

questions.question: The managing director did not pay his staff last month ...?
questions.explanation: For negative statements, tag questions flip: negative statement gets positive tag. 'Did not pay...did he?' The tag confirms the negative meaning and invites agreement.

questions.question: Although he is in all respects a poor student, he has managed to buy a tape recorder to improve his .... of French
questions.explanation: 'Mastery of French' means complete control and proficiency. He bought the tape recorder to improve his level of skill. 'Speaking' is narrower; 'mastering' is a verb, not a noun here.

questions.question: Granted you are my senior by one year, you cannot .... me around as if I were your servant
questions.explanation: 'Order me around' is the idiom meaning to boss someone—to command them. 'Control' is similar but less colloquial. 'Dribble' makes no sense; 'compel' is too formal.

questions.question: .... mark an important step in the history of industrial revolution
options: The invention of the locomotive engine
questions.explanation: The locomotive engine's invention was pivotal in the Industrial Revolution—it revolutionized transportation and industry. 'Discovery of locomotion' is vague; 'process of locomotion' is the ongoing use, not the breakthrough.

questions.question: Because the referee was partial and his opponent rough, the loser of the wrestling match received .... cheers than the winner
questions.explanation: A biased referee and rough opponent mean the loser gets sympathy. 'Many more cheers' is the comparison—he received more cheers than the winner, which is unusual but makes sense given the circumstances.

questions.question: The boxer was highly rated .... his doggedness and ability to absorb heavy punches
questions.explanation: 'Rated for' means evaluated or valued for specific qualities. 'He was rated for his doggedness'—meaning he earned recognition because of that trait.

questions.question: Many lives are lost on Nigerian roads .... lack of consideration for other road users by many crazy drivers
questions.explanation: 'Through lack of consideration' is the cause—lives are lost through (by means of) this lack. It's the instrument/cause. 'Owing to' and 'in view of' work similarly but 'through' is most direct.

questions.question: The man no longer feared his opponent after their spokesman had assured him that they were well disposed .... him
questions.explanation: 'Well disposed toward' means favorable, friendly attitudes. 'The opponent's camp is well-disposed toward him' means they're friendly. 'Toward' shows direction/target of the disposition.

questions.question: My little boy is hyper-active and he is therefore prone .... accidents
questions.explanation: 'Prone to' is the correct idiom—it means susceptible or likely to experience something. 'He is prone to accidents' means he tends to have them because he's hyperactive.

questions.question: His horrible high-pitched laugh .... on my nerves
questions.explanation: 'Gets on my nerves' is a common idiom, but here 'jars on my nerves' is correct—'jar' means to disturb or grate. His laugh bothers and irritates you by striking your nerves the wrong way.

questions.question: The train rattled along .... full speed
questions.explanation: 'At full speed' is the correct phrase. 'At' indicates a state or rate. 'Moving at full speed' is standard. 'In full speed' isn't used; 'with full speed' is awkward.

questions.question: You would have thought that somebody would have informed me ....
questions.explanation: 'Wouldn't you' completes the tag question correctly. 'You would have thought...wouldn't you?'—a polite challenge to the listener's assumption. It fits the hypothetical structure.

questions.question: If this had happened to you, what ......?
questions.explanation: The condition is past ('had happened'), so the result must also be past: 'would you have done.' This is third conditional—hypothetical past events and their imaginary outcomes.

questions.question: What a relief that the strike .... when it did!
questions.explanation: 'Ended' is simple past—the strike finished at that moment. 'What a relief' expresses past relief about what already happened. Other forms don't fit the past timeframe.

questions.question: We should take care .... the robbers come back
questions.explanation: 'In case' is the correct phrase—it means 'if something happens' or 'for protection against.' We should take care 'in case' the robbers return. It's a conditional clause.

questions.question: By the time the rainy season ended, all the young cocoa trees .... in size
questions.explanation: 'By the time...ended' is past perfect context. 'Had doubled' is past perfect—it describes something completed before another past event. The trees finished doubling before the season ended.

questions.question: There were so many children ....
options: that she couldn't feed them all
that she could feed them all
more than she could feed them all
questions.explanation: 'So many...that' is a cause-effect structure. 'There were so many children that she couldn't feed them all' = the large number caused her inability to feed everyone.

questions.question: Isn't it high time you .... your office?
questions.explanation: 'High time' is followed by past tense: 'high time you left' (or 'did leave'). It's polite criticism—you should have left already. 'Left' emphasizes the past recommendation.

questions.question: In West Africa the .... of sickle cell is about 25 percent
questions.explanation: 'Incidence' (pronounced in-SID-ence) means frequency or rate of occurrence—here, how often sickle cell appears. 'Incident' is an event; 'accident' is unintentional; 'incense' is anger or a scent.

questions.question: We got to the hall after the play ....
questions.explanation: 'After the play had started' is past perfect—describing something that happened before 'we got there.' The play began first, then we arrived. Past perfect shows this sequence.

questions.question: The plaintiff made very cogent submissions to the trail judge
questions.explanation: 'Cogent' means persuasive and compelling. The plaintiff's arguments were strong. The opposite role would be the defendant. This question asks: who is opposite to plaintiff? Answer: defendant.

questions.question: We visited the home of one boy. That's the boy I mean
options: That's the boy whom we visited his home
That's the boy whose home we visited
That's the boy to whose home we visited
That's the boy whom we visited the home
That's the boy the home of whom we visited
questions.explanation: 'Whose home we visited' is correct relative clause structure. 'Whose' shows possession; 'we visited his home' becomes 'we visited whose home.' It's the most natural paraphrase.

questions.question: The dog was limping. It appeared that one of its legs might have been injured
options: The dog was limping as if it had an injured leg
The dog was limping as it had an injured leg
The dog was limping so it had an injured leg
The dog was limping so as it had an injured leg
The dog was limping for it had an injured leg
questions.explanation: 'As if' introduces a hypothetical resemblance: 'as if it had an injured leg' = it looked like it might have an injury. 'As if' shows uncertain appearance, matching 'might have been.'

questions.question: I can walk that distance and have done so many times
options: I am used to walk that distance
I used to walk that distance
I used to walking that distance
I am used to walking that distance
questions.explanation: 'Am used to walking' is the correct form. 'Used to' = past habit; 'am used to' = accustomed to. Here, 'I am accustomed to walking' = I do it regularly and comfortably.

questions.question: For all he cared, his parent might have been dead for years
options: although concern about his parent, he had been unable to find out if they were alive
He cared for no one except his parents, and it was possible that they had died long before
he did not care whether his parents were alive or had died long before
He was concerned about everyone not just his parents, who might have died long before
He cared very much for his parents even though they were dead
questions.explanation: 'For all he cared' = 'as far as he cared' = 'he didn't care at all.' The meaning: he was so indifferent that whether parents were alive or dead didn't matter to him. Complete apathy.
